title: Food Skills & Psychological Resilience Predict Gluten-Free Diet Adherence in Adults with Celiac Disease
authors_full: Mehul Shekar, BS | Sandra Mayol-Kreiser, PhD, RD | Ding-Geng Chen, PhD | Carol S. Johnston, PhD, RD
authors_short: Mehul Shekar BS  Sandra Mayol-Kreiser RD  Ding-Geng Chen PhD  Carol S. Johnston

summary.hook: This cross-sectional study used an anonymous online survey of adults with self-reported clinician-diagnosed celiac disease (n=327) to examine whether food skills and psychological...
summary.summary_30s: This cross-sectional study used an anonymous online survey of adults with self-reported clinician-diagnosed celiac disease (n=327) to examine whether food skills and psychological resilience are associated with gluten-free diet (GFD) adherence.

key_numbers: Adults (≥18 years) with self-reported celiac disease diagnosis.
Meal planning + resilience explained 12.3% of variance in GFD adherence scores.
Study included 327 adults (≥18) with self-reported doctor-diagnosed celiac disease.
This cross-sectional study used an anonymous online survey of adults with self-reported clinician-diagnosed celiac...
takeaways: Correlational analysis identified exploratory variables linked to GFD adherence: food skills (meal planning, food preparation, food safety), resilience, and age.
Multiple linear regression identified meal planning and resilience as significant explanatory variables for GFD adherence.
The model explained 12.3% of the variance in adherence scores.
